title: Presigned Browser Uploads, POST Policies, and CORS · Execution Trace: Presigned Put Header Parity
summary: A browser receives a broad week-long upload URL, changes signed headers, overwrites a shared key, and mistakes CORS success for storage authorization. Predict the representation, storage, trust, cost, and recovery evidence before inspecting a model. Planned artifact: Add and verify the presigned put header parity increment in least-privilege direct-upload session contract. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory, changed-case practice, causal debugging, and evidence defense.
objectives: Presign PUT with exact region, expiry, key, checksum, content type, encryption, and required header parity.
Create a one-object upload intent with tenant, generated key, expected size, type, checksum, expiry, status, and replay policy before signing.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

steps.title: S3, CloudFront, and Go Predict Presigned Put Header Parity · Theory Presigned Put Header Parity
steps.instruction:
theory case — A theory Go file-delivery team handles case 4796 during a cost-anomaly investigation. Build deterministic Go evidence to finalize a direct upload against intent while separating CORS from IAM authorization; cap bytes, time, attempts, concurrency, and spend before starting work; then double concurrent transfers and keep process resource limits intact. Browser code uses pure values and state transitions only. AWS SDK packages, networks, S3, CloudFront, KMS, IAM, WAF, databases, credentials, host state, race and leak checks, load, faults, restore, billing, and production behavior require explicit authorized transfer gates.

Before reading, predict which action preserves the Presigned Put Header Parity contract for the supplied data.
